"If there be therefore any consolation in Christ, if any comfort of love, if any fellowship of the Spirit, if any bowels and mercies," The witnesses note Paul's tender, fourfold appeal for unity. Chrysostom notes there is nothing more affectionate than a spiritual teacher, surpassing the kindness of any natural father; see how earnestly, vehemently, and with how much sympathy Paul speaks, exhorting the Philippians concerning concord, the cause of all good things. The witnesses note Paul beginning his plea for unity with a fourfold "if" — any consolation in Christ, any comfort of love, any fellowship of the Spirit, any bowels and mercies; the heaping of motives to press for concord — that Paul, with the warmth of a loving father, gathers up every tender argument he can to move the Philippians to unity: the encouragement they have in Christ, the comfort that flows from love, the fellowship they share in the Spirit, and the tender affections and compassions of the heart, as if to say, by all these things, be of one mind; if there be any consolation in Christ. The witnesses note any consolation in Christ, any comfort of love. The witnesses note any fellowship of the Spirit, any bowels and mercies.

2:

Gill notes Paul's joy in them would be fulfilled (filled up, made complete) by their being of one mind, which is a great part of a minister's comfort in his people. 3:

The witnesses note the warning against strife and the call to lowly esteem of others. Gill notes that "being in the form of God" means the Father; Christ is the brightness of his glory and the express image of his person — not any shape or accidental form (there being no accidents in God), but his very nature and essence, for he is the Jehovah, I am what I am. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ, being in the form of God, thought it not robbery to be equal with God; the true and proper deity of Christ — that Paul lays the foundation of the great self-humbling in the height from which Christ stooped: he subsisted "in the form of God" (possessing the very nature, glory, and essence of Godhead), and counted his being "equal with God" no usurpation or robbery, but his own rightful and natural possession, so that the One who emptied himself was none other than the eternal, true God; thought it not robbery to be equal with God. 7:
"But made himself of no reputation, and took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men:" The witnesses note the self-emptying of Christ in becoming man. Gill notes "made himself of no reputation" is literally "emptied himself" — not of his fulness of grace, nor of the perfections of his divine nature (which were not diminished, all the fulness of the Godhead dwelling in him bodily), but by taking the form of a servant and the likeness of men. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Christ emptied himself, taking the form of a servant and the likeness of men; the great self-emptying (kenosis) in the incarnation — that Christ, who was in the form of God, "emptied himself," not by laying aside or diminishing his deity (which he could not), but by veiling its glory and taking to himself what he had not before: the "form of a servant" (the real condition of a bond-servant) and "the likeness of men" (a true and complete human nature), so that the infinite God, without ceasing to be God, became a lowly man and servant; made himself of no reputation, took the form of a servant. The witnesses note made himself of no reputation. The witnesses note took upon him the form of a servant, and was made in the likeness of men.

8:

Gill notes Paul proceeds to the exaltation of Christ for the encouragement of meek and humble souls — that whereas Christ, who so demeaned himself, was highly exalted, so all who imitate him in lowliness shall be exalted in due time, for whoso humbleth himself shall be exalted. 10:
